**[P0] Coverage Election vs Composite Scoring** — design-extract uses a binary coverage gate: tokens appearing across >= N canonical pages are 'elected' as system tokens; single-page tokens are demoted or dropped. Our `classifyColorStability()` (cluster.ts:400-435) uses a composite 5-signal score with weighted adds/subtracts, where pageCoverage is just one of five signals (30pts max). On single-page extractions, pageCoverage is always 1.0, making the coverage signal meaningless and allowing gradient colors used only in a hero section (frequency=6, gradientColor-only) to score high enough to escape L4 classification — this is the likely L4 leakage mechanism. The raw OKLCH deltaE < 3 clustering (cluster.ts:698-724) is orthogonal: it merges near-neighbor colors but doesn't prevent leakage on its own.

**[P0] DTCG $type/$value as Provenance Anchor** — W3C DTCG 2025.10 mandates every token as `{"$type": "...", "$value": ...}` where $type is a hard schema slot (color, dimension, shadow, typography, gradient, cubicBezier, etc.). This creates a type-lattice: a DESIGN.md claim about shadows must cite a `$type:"shadow"` token, a claim about gradients must cite a `$type:"gradient"` token. The $type is enforced at token construction time — you cannot emit a token without declaring its type. Our current flat tokens.json shape (e.g., `{hex, frequency, usedAs}`) has no type field. Our validate.ts only checks hex existence (validate.ts:57-93) — hex is an insufficient anchor because: (a) gradients aren't hex, (b) shadows have hex components but the claim is about shadow semantics, not the hex color inside, (c) two semantically distinct tokens can share a hex after OKLCH clustering. DTCG's typed slots are strictly stronger than hex-only sentinel tracing — they enable validator rules like: 'every shadow claim in DESIGN.md §6 must match a $type:"shadow" token by value hash.'

**[P1] DTCG Token Shape as Single Highest-Value Adoption** — The DTCG `$type`/`$value` format is the single highest-value architecture to adopt because: (1) it is standards-track (W3C Community Group, 20+ reference implementations), making our output immediately consumable by other tools (Style Dictionary, Tokens Studio, design-extract's own emitters); (2) it directly fixes our provenance problem — $type creates a hard schema anchor that validate.ts can enforce; (3) it is a prerequisite for composite/semantic tokens (composites reference primitives by $id, which DTCG standardizes); (4) it is the format design-extract already emits, aligning us with the stronger competitor; (5) the change is localized to 3 files (types.ts, cluster.ts, validate.ts) + 1 resource doc (writing_style_guide.md), with no changes to the Playwright extraction pipeline.

**[P1] Semantic/Composite Token Vocabulary** — design-extract outputs three token layers: (1) primitive (raw values: hex, px, ms), (2) semantic (role-named: `color.surface.primary`, `shadow.card.elevated`), (3) composite (multi-value tokens referencing primitives: `shadow.card.elevated` = `{offsetX, offsetY, blur, spread, color: ref.color.surface.shadow}`). This creates a CONSTRAINED VOCABULARY for the AI writer: instead of free prose like 'the shadows create a floating effect with soft edges,' the writer references `shadow.card.elevated` which has a known, verifiable $value. The vocabulary space is bounded to only elected tokens, preventing invention. Our current DESIGN.md writer has no such vocabulary — every section is free prose with only hex codes as fact anchors. The writer can invent concepts like 'the design uses a layered floating card system' without any token supporting 'layered' or 'floating.'

**[P0] Section 0 (Brand Context) — personality fabrication** — Requires 3-5 adjectives each with 'parenthetical rationale tied to an observable design decision'. When the site has no strong personality signals, the AI fabricates rationales. A 9-section schema would collapse this to a metadata header (brand name, URL, pages crawled) without mandating personality interpretation.

**[P0] Section 1 (Visual Theme & Atmosphere) — primary fabrication vector** — Requires: unique opening sentence, 3 prose paragraphs with embedded CSS, 2-4 'Named design principles', comparative framing ('Unlike most systems...'), 8-12 Key Characteristics. The Vercel example opens 'the visual thesis of developer infrastructure made invisible — a design system so restrained it borders on philosophical'; Stripe: 'the gold standard of fintech design'. Both are LLM-authored editorial, not extracted data. When CSS signals are thin, this section forces the AI to invent philosophical framing (cf. 'gradient-as-depth' fabrication). 9-section schemas (DesignMD, Open Design) either omit this entirely or reduce it to a value-table of key characteristics without mandatory prose.

**[P0] Section 6 (Depth & Elevation) — forced philosophical framing** — Requires 'Named principle' (chromatic-depth, shadow-as-border, luminance-stepping, etc.) plus 50-100 word 'Contrast with conventional approach' paragraph. The Vercel example writes a 90-word shadow philosophy paragraph. This forces editorial interpretation of raw shadow values. 9-section schemas list shadow values in a table without naming a 'philosophy'.

**[P1] Input-side filtering (Superposition/CSS Stats) — cleaner-input anti-fabrication** — Superposition excludes unused styles BEFORE the writer sees them. CSS Stats clusters colors by OCCURRENCE COUNT, making frequency the credibility signal. Our pipeline extracts ALL tokens (including one-off L4 values) and hands them to the AI with mandated narrative sections. The AI sees noise and must fill word counts — worst-case combination. The 9-section tools' value-table approach means the writer never needs to 'interpret' thin data into prose.

**[P1] Section 6.5 (Motion System) — forced philosophy when no data exists** — Spec explicitly requires a motion section even when no motion data exists, and mandates: 'Motion philosophy' sentence + recommendations for what to implement. This is a direct fabrication mandate — the AI must invent motion philosophy from nothing. The Vercel example simply lists duration/timing values without philosophy.

**[P1] Section 7 (Content & Voice) — vibe paragraph fabrication** — Requires 'Vibe Paragraph' (2-3 sentences capturing brand personality through copy) and tone descriptors with real quotes. The Vercel example writes: 'The absence of exclamation marks and emoji signals a brand that lets its product speak rather than performing enthusiasm.' This is editorial inference, not extracted data. A 9-section schema would list observed copy patterns (capitalization, button labels, emoji policy) as value-tables without mandating a 'vibe' narrative.

**[P2] Section 3 (Typography Named Strategies) — architectural benefit fabrication** — Requires 3-5 'Named Strategies' each with bold name, values, and 'architectural benefit'. The Stripe example: 'Light weight as signature: Weight 300 at display sizes... Where others use 600-700 to command attention, Stripe uses lightness as luxury.' The 'architectural benefit' is editorial inference. A 9-section schema lists the typography scale as a table without mandating strategy names.

**[P0] Deterministic value-table generation from typed tokens** — Current pipeline: AI reads tokens.json and writes DESIGN.md value-tables (typography scale, color palette table, shadow/radius tables, component matrix). This is the primary hallucination vector — the validator's checkPhantomColors catches fabricated hex codes, but a fabricated fontSize, fontWeight, or lineHeight in a typography table passes undetected. Diez's DocsGen generates token documentation deterministically from typed token data — zero inference, zero fabrication. Applying this: Sections 2 (Color Palette & Roles), 3 (Typography Rules), 4 (Component Stylings value-tables), 6 (Depth & Elevation tables), 6.5 (Motion System tables), 17 (Design Tokens Dictionary) can all become deterministic formatters over typed tokens. Sections 0 (Brand Context), 1 (Visual Theme), 5 (Layout Principles prose), 7 (Content & Voice), 8 (Do's/Don'ts), 9-16 require AI authorial judgment and stay AI-written.

**[P0] DTCG $type/$value adoption in tokens.json** — Current tokens.json is a flat bag of category arrays (colorTokens[], typographyLevels[], shadowTokens[], radiusTokens[]) with extraction metadata (frequency, confidence, usedAs). DTCG wraps each token as {"$type":"color","$value":"#533afd","$description":"..."}. Concretely improves: (1) claim provenance — each token self-declares its type, so a dimension can't slip into a color slot; (2) validator binding — validate.ts currently uses ad-hoc heuristics (regex hex matching, backtick font-name detection) that could become type-gated assertions; (3) downstream emit — Style Dictionary, Terrazzo, and Theo all consume DTCG natively, enabling deterministic multi-platform output without transform code. What breaks: cluster.ts (1742 lines) builds flat arrays — needs a DTCG wrapper layer (~200 LOC); types.ts needs DTCG-aware interfaces; validate.ts's checkPhantomColors/checkUnknownFonts become type-filtered passes. Extraction code (extract.ts, dom-collector.ts) is unaffected — it feeds cluster.ts, not tokens.json directly.

**[P1] Aliasing for semantic color roles vs free authorial naming** — Current DESIGN.md color roles are AI-inferred: the AI reads tokens.colorTokens and assigns semantic labels (primary, secondary, surface, text) based on frequency and usage heuristics. This is fragile — the same extraction may produce different role assignments on different runs. DTCG aliasing creates explicit semantic mappings: {"color.primary": {"$value": "{color.brand.500}", "$type": "color"}}. Concretely: tokens.json could include a 'semanticAliases' section that maps role names to extracted token indices, computed deterministically from frequency+usage rules (e.g., highest-frequency bgColor = surface, highest-chromatic bgColor = primary). The AI then uses aliases instead of re-inferring each time.

**[P1] Style Dictionary transform/format pipeline separation** — Current pipeline is extract→write (monolithic). Style Dictionary's model: source tokens → transforms (name/attribute/value math) → formats (CSS vars, iOS, Android, JS). Separation means each stage is independently testable and replaceable. For sk-design-md-generator: 'extraction → typed tokens → formatted DESIGN.md' is cleaner than 'extraction → AI-write-everything'. The transform stage becomes a no-op for the primary path (values are already measured, not derived), but the FORMAT stage is where deterministic table generation lives. This also opens a future path: if tokens.json is DTCG-shaped, any Style Dictionary formatter can consume it for non-DESIGN.md outputs (CSS custom properties file, Tailwind config, etc.).

**[P0] hybrid-static-computed-var-resolution** — Our dom-collector.ts stylesheet walk captures --var names and their raw authored values (e.g. var(--color-primary)), but never resolves var() chains to their computed values. cluster.ts:662-675 can only cross-reference a CSS variable to a color token when the raw value is a direct color string (hex/rgb/hsl). CSS variables storing indirection (var() references, fallback chains) produce zero matches in the cross-reference loop, meaning var-named tokens appear in cssVariables[] but have no colorToken linkage. The downstream DESIGN.md carries variable names without resolved hex provenance. A hybrid approach — walk stylesheets for declaration metadata (name, selector, raw value) PLUS resolve each --var via getComputedStyle(document.documentElement).getPropertyValue(name) in the browser runtime — closes this gap: every CSS variable gets a name + resolved-value pair. This is the single highest-fidelity improvement available.

**[P1] occurrence-band-pre-clustering** — Our cluster.ts:698-724 applies greedy OKLCH perceptual clustering (deltaE<3) sorted by frequency-desc: the highest-frequency color always wins as the cluster representative, and any lower-frequency color within deltaE<3 merges into it. This is purely perceptual-space and can merge a true brand color (#0033cc, freq=2000) with a one-off decorative blue (#0033ca, freq=4) that happens to sit just 1.8 deltaE away, losing the occurrence signal that would keep them distinct. CSS Stats' occurrence-based clustering does the inverse — it groups by how many times a value appears before applying any perceptual merge. A combined two-pass approach would: (1) bucket colors by occurrence frequency percentile (top-quartile → high-confidence brand/system candidates; bottom-quartile → suspect one-offs), (2) perceptual-cluster within each band independently, (3) cross-band: only merge low-freq into high-freq when deltaE < 1.0 (very tight). This preserves the occurrence signal that our current perceptual-only approach discards.

**[P1] role-typed-css-var-dedup** — Our CSS variable extraction (dom-collector.ts:105-143) is a flat concat: every --custom-property from :root/html/body/theme selectors is dumped into one array with no grouping by role (color, spacing, font, shadow, radius). The consistency analysis in cluster.ts:1423-1445 does per-name cross-page consistency but no structural grouping. get-custom-properties-style extraction would group by name prefix (--color-* → colorTokens, --spacing-* → spacingSystem, --font-* → typographyLevels, --shadow-* → shadowTokens, --radius-* → radiusTokens) and deduplicate at the selector level (same name on same selector → keep last; same name on different selectors → record both). This maps directly to our 17-section DESIGN.md structure and eliminates the noisy mix of unrelated variables that currently floods the cssVariables[] array. Additionally, our scope is too narrow — we only check :root/html/body/theme, missing component-level custom properties (.button { --btn-radius: 8px }) which are common in shadow-DOM-free component libraries.

**[P2] static-property-type-discovery** — project-wallace/css-analyzer does static CSS parse to categorize every declaration by property type (colors under color/background-color/border-color, sizes under font-size/width/height, etc.) and emits occurrence counts for each distinct value AS-FOUND. Our css-analyzer.ts already has a full css-tree AST parse (line 487) but only extracts pseudo-classes, media queries, transitions, and animations — it ignores the vast bulk of property declarations for token discovery. A static-property-type pass would discover tokens that never render at runtime (unused theme variants, disabled states, print stylesheets) and provide a secondary cross-validation signal against the computed-style census: if a color appears 200 times in static CSS but only 3 times in computed elements, it's likely a special-state token (focus ring, error state) that deserves inclusion.

**[P0] Multi-file typed collections + Manifest (Penpot, Tokens Studio)** — Penpot exports one JSON file per mode with a Manifest.json linking them; Tokens Studio splits tokens by collection (color/type/space/etc.) with a manifest registry. Both make each token slot individually addressable by file+key rather than requiring a linear scan of a monolithic array. Our tokens.json currently dumps all token types (colors, typography, shadows, radii, spacing, components, gradients, breakpoints, a11y, darkMode, cssVariables) into one 4600-line flat file. A writer must traverse the entire colorTokens array to find a specific hex; there is no stable key to cite.

**[P1] Composite typed tokens (Specify typography/shadow/gradient objects)** — Specify's SDTF represents typography as a single typed composite: `{ "$type": "typography", "$value": { "fontFamily": "Inter", "fontSize": "16px", "fontWeight": 400, "lineHeight": 1.5, "letterSpacing": "0%" } }`. Similarly, shadow tokens are structured `{ offsetX, offsetY, blur, spread, color }` objects, not raw CSS strings. Our tokens.json typographyLevels keeps fontFamily/fontSize/fontWeight as separate flat fields with no composite token identity; shadowTokens stores raw CSS `box-shadow` strings; gradients are flat `{ type, value, elementTag, location }` entries. A writer must re-parse these to reassemble prose descriptions, which is the assembly step where fabrication risk enters.

A color 'used mostly as text' (textColor:294, borderColor:735) has ambiguous identity — the writer doesn't know if this is a text token or a border token. The colorRelationships.scales detect OKLCH hue-groups but don't name them. CSS variable names (cssVariableNames[]) are collected but never used to create alias chains. There is no semantic→primitive reference layer.

**[P1] extract-design-system Skill Architecture (Prompt-Driven Grounding)** — extract-design-system (arvindrk) is an AI-agent SKILL — meaning its entire extraction logic lives IN THE PROMPT, not in separate tool code. The skill instructs the AI to: read the page's computed CSS → classify by W3C DTCG type → emit tokens.json + tokens.css. There is NO interpretive WRITE phase — the output is machine tokens, not prose. Dembrandt takes the opposite architecture: pure Node CLI, static+computed CSS hybrid, zero AI involvement. Our architecture is hybrid: TypeScript CLI extracts → AI writes DESIGN.md prose → TypeScript CLI validates. The key vulnerability in our architecture is the AI-operated WRITE phase — the same LLM that hallucinates is tasked with composing prose. extract-design-system's prompt-driven architecture avoids this by never asking the LLM to write prose — it outputs structured data only. Dembrandt avoids it by being fully programmatic. The borrowable pattern is NOT to eliminate the WRITE phase (the DESIGN.md prose IS our value proposition), but to front-load token constraints MORE aggressively in the WRITE prompt so the LLM writes grounded prose. Currently, the WRITE prompt template (design_md_prompt_template.md) has 6 cardinal rules but only #1 addresses hallucination directly. The template should include a 'Claim Evidence Required' rule: 'Every conceptual claim (principle name, metaphor, comparative framing) in Sections 1, 6, and 7 MUST be followed by a parenthetical citing >=2 token values or frequency ratios from tokens.json. A claim without parenthetical evidence is a fabrication.' Additionally, Dembrandt's static+computed hybrid approach (parsing both stylesheet CSS AND runtime computed values) produces more complete tokens than our computed-only approach — CSS custom properties defined in stylesheets but not applied to elements would be missed by our Playwright computed-style extraction.

**[P1] Multi-Agent Output Structuring (Open Design)** — Open Design's DESIGN.md is consumed by 4+ coding agents (Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, OpenCode) with a DETERMINISTIC RE-RENDER schema — the same input produces the same output regardless of which agent reads it. This is achieved by a structured section contract where each section's content type is constrained. Our DESIGN.md is consumed by sk-code, sk-design-interface, and potentially other agents, but our prose-heavy sections (1, 7, 0) produce different interpretations per agent: Claude Code might extract 'shadow-as-border' as a technique; Codex might extract it as a design philosophy; Cursor might parse it as a component pattern. Our Section 13 (Agent Prompt Guide) already solves this by providing self-contained copy-paste prompts — the prompts work identically for any agent. But Sections 1-12 are freeform prose with no structured consumption contract. Evidence: the Vercel example's Section 1 includes claims like 'The Geist design system treats the interface like a compiler treats code' — this is a compelling metaphor for Claude but may confuse a code-generation agent that needs parseable rules. The fix: add a structured 'Agent-Oriented Summary' block at the TOP of each prose-heavy section, with key-value pairs that any agent can parse: 'Primary font: Geist. Headline weight: 600. Headline tracking: -2.88px at 48px. Shadow technique: box-shadow as border (0px 0px 0px 1px rgba(0,0,0,0.08)).' This creates a dual-layer document: structured header for agents, freeform prose for humans.

**[P2] REFERENCES Section Gap (Open Design)** — Open Design's 9-section DESIGN.md includes a REFERENCES section for citations and further reading. Our DESIGN.md has no references section. The SKILL.md references 10+ resource docs (design_md_format.md, writing_style_guide.md, anti_patterns.md, etc.) but none of these make it into the DESIGN.md output. A consumer reading our DESIGN.md has no provenance trail for where the values came from beyond the 'Generated: 2026-04-05' header. A REFERENCES section would cite: the source URL(s), the extraction tool version, the token stability class definitions, and any external standards referenced (WCAG level, DTCG format). This is lower priority because it adds provenance without directly preventing fabrication, but it improves trust and debuggability for multi-agent consumers.

**[P2] Section Count Inflation — Spec vs Reality** — The design_md_format.md specifies 14 core + 1 half-section + 4 optional = up to 19 sections. But ALL four gold-standard examples (Vercel: 10 sections, Stripe: 9 sections, Linear: 9 sections, Supabase: ~9 sections) output only 9-10 sections. The spec demands sections (0 Brand Context, 7 Content & Voice, 9 Accessibility Contract, 11 State Matrix, 12 Iconography) that are NEVER written in practice. This creates two problems: (1) the section count claim ('17-section DESIGN.md') is misleading to consumers who expect all 17; (2) the WRITE phase is contractually obligated to produce sections it cannot fulfill from tokens.json, creating fabrication pressure. The 'required' designation for sections 7 and 11-12 in a single-page extraction where no content text or component states were sampled forces the LLM to invent content.

**[P0] B13: findInteraction drops real interaction data** — Confirmed. interactionLookup keys use capture.componentType (e.g., 'link', 'button', 'tab') from classifyComponent(). But findInteraction constructs the primary key as `${el.tag}|${el.className}|${el.tag}` using el.tag for the componentType slot (cluster.ts:1294). For <a> elements (tag='a', componentType='link') and role-based components (tag='div', componentType='button'), the primary lookup never matches. The linear-scan fallback (cluster.ts:1297-1301) checks el.className.includes(cap.element.classes) — this fails when the representative element has fewer classes than the captured element (common since only one element per first-class-prefix is captured). When both fail, hoverDiff/focusVisibleDiff/focusDiff/activeDiff/disabledStyle are all set to null (cluster.ts:1347-1351) despite real data existing in InteractionCapture.

**[P0] B2: extractA11yAsync never wired** — Confirmed. extractA11yAsync (a11y-extract.ts:361) exports tabOrder/langAttribute/skipLinkDetected/altTextCoverage but is never imported or called in extract.ts. Only the synchronous extractA11y is called (extract.ts:445). The browser is already closed (extract.ts:422) before a11y assembly, so async capture never runs. Four fields are silently missing from A11yTokens output; the writer must invent them.

**[P1] B14: classifyShadow duplicated with drift risk** — Confirmed. classifyShadow is defined twice in cluster.ts: once as an exported function (cluster.ts:374-396) and once as an inline function inside clusterTokens (cluster.ts:1047-1072). Both implementations are currently identical. The inline copy (line 1047) shadows the exported copy and is the one actually used for shadow token classification (line 1090). If a maintainer fixes a classification bug in the exported version, the inline copy would drift, silently leaving shadow classification broken. Zero-blur border-shadows are correctly separated from elevation because spread>0 gates the border-shadow branch, and the blind fallthrough returns 'elevation' which is harmless for zero-blur shadows.

**[P1] B16: global-min touch/font skewed by hidden elements** — Confirmed with nuance. Two independent minTouchTarget/minFontSize computations exist: one in a11y-extract.ts (lines 306-338, the one that ships to output) and one in cluster.ts (lines 1532-1568, overwritten at extract.ts:469 so dead code). The a11y-extract.ts version already has reasonable guards: minTouchTarget filters to interactive tags (button/a/input/select/textarea) and excludes zero-area elements; minFontSize requires textContent.trim(). However, both miss visibility filtering — a 1px-wide interactive element hidden via CSS can produce a misleading minimum that flags false WCAG violations. The cluster.ts dead-code version is worse: minFontSize scans all elements with positive parsePxValue without checking textContent or visibility.

Evidence: a11y-extract.ts:306-325 (minTouchTarget filters interactive tags but not visibility), a11y-extract.ts:327-338 (minFontSize requires textContent but not visibility), cluster.ts:1532-1568 (dead-code duplicate with weaker filtering), extract.ts:469 (cluster a11yTokens overwritten by extractA11y result)

Evidence: a11y-extract.ts:361-391 (exported but unused), extract.ts:19 (import misses extractA11yAsync), extract.ts:422 (browser.close before a11y assembly), extract.ts:445 (only sync extractA11y called)

**[P0] Element-level provenance lost at clustering** — dom-collector.ts:186-266 captures full per-element computed styles (tag, className, rect, structuralRegion, nearestLandmark) but cluster.ts:576-607 aggregates by RGBA key via addColor(), discarding all selector/element provenance. ColorToken only retains cssVariableNames and sourcePages (URL-level), not which selectors/elements produced each token. A 'where-used' anchor per token (e.g., 'button.btn-primary, nav .cta-link') would let DESIGN.md Section 4 cite concrete selectors alongside frequency, which is strictly more informative than aggregate frequency alone.

**[P1] Confidence as promotion gate, not just label** — ColorToken.confidence (types.ts:424) is 'high'|'medium'|'low' based on frequency/pageCoverage. But low-confidence tokens are still promoted to DESIGN.md main sections if they pass the L1/L2 stability gate. The stability classifier (cluster.ts:400-435) and confidence field are computed independently — a token can be L2-stable but low-confidence. The tools described (MiroMiro, StyleSniff) let humans curate; the automated equivalent is refusing to promote low-evidence tokens to main sections.

**[P1] Structural-region-aware confidence scoring** — cluster.ts:400-435 classifyColorStability() uses page coverage, usage dimensions, CSS variable presence, frequency, and chromaticity — but ignores WHERE in the DOM the token lives. A color appearing only in footer/nav (infrastructure regions) should score higher than one appearing only in random divs. The structuralRegion field is already captured per element in dom-collector.ts:60-74 but never fed into stability classification.

**[P1] Value-only templated export for agent consumption** — DESIGN.md is a 17-section prose-heavy document. For agent consumption (e.g., an AI building components), the prose is token-waste — the agent needs only the values. A flat JSON/YAML export with zero prose (just token name → value mappings, grouped by category) would reduce context consumption by ~70% while preserving all actionable data. The data already exists in tokens.json but is raw; a curated value-only export would mirror DESIGN.md's structure without the narrative.

**[P2] Gradient pixel sampling gap** — cluster.ts:244-261 extractGradientColors() parses color stops from CSS gradient strings via regex. This misses: (a) colors that are browser-interpolated between stops (not in the CSS text), (b) gradients applied via background-image on pseudo-elements where the computed value may differ from authored, (c) CSS gradient functions not matching the regex patterns. MiroMiro and Sip sample the RENDERED pixel, not the CSS string — they see the actual composited output.

**[P2] Opacity-composited color precision gap** — dom-collector.ts:207 reads backgroundColor as a computed value (often rgba with alpha). cluster.ts:579-580 skips fully transparent (a===0) but does NOT composite semi-transparent colors onto their effective background. A color like rgba(0,0,0,0.5) on a white background renders as #808080, but the extraction stores the rgba form. The hex conversion (rgbaToHex) discards alpha entirely. This means two visually identical colors (one opaque, one semi-transparent on white) produce different tokens.

**[P0] Step 5 — Role Naming (LLM)** — The 6-step methodology maps as: (1) Playwright crawl [DETERMINISTIC], (2) computed CSS extraction [DETERMINISTIC], (3) OKLCH clustering deltaE<3 + frequency dedup [DETERMINISTIC], (4) L1-L4 stability scoring [DETERMINISTIC], (5) role assignment via LLM per color_role_taxonomy.md heuristics [FABRICATION RISK], (6) DESIGN.md composition with personality sentences + named principles + comparative framing [FABRICATION RISK]. Step 5 is where the LLM interprets usage frequency, context, and CSS variable names to assign semantic roles (primary/secondary/accent/border etc.). Even with the 5-tier priority heuristic (CSS var name > framework convention > usage frequency+context > visual position > hue), the LLM still exercises judgment on "usage frequency + context combination" and the anti-pattern AP-03 (semantic misattribution) proves this failure mode is real — calling blue 'primary' when it appears on 3% of elements while gray dominates 62% of text.

**[P0] Step 6 — Narrative Prose (LLM)** — The WRITE phase injects LLM-generated narrative across ALL 14+ sections: personality sentences on every color entry (writing_style_guide.md §6: 'One sentence about why this specific color was chosen and what it feels like'), named design principles ('chromatic depth', 'whispered authority' — all LLM-invented), comparative framing ('where most systems use X, this system uses Y'), metaphors ('content emerges from darkness like starlight'), and atmosphere paragraphs. These are ALL fabrication — the LLM generates claims about design intent from raw hex values. The consensus in design-system methodology is that a design-system doc should be value-tables + minimal factual annotation, not prose essays. The gold-standard examples (stripe, vercel, linear, supabase) ARE prose-heavy but were generated by the same LLM methodology and are thus subject to the same fabrication risk.

**[P1] Step 4 — System Inference (L1-L4)** — The standard methodology's Step 4 (token vs incidental) is implemented as L1-L4 stability classification in cluster.ts:classifyColorStability(). The score uses 5 signals — page_coverage (0.8/0.5/0.2), usage dimensions, CSS variable presence, absolute frequency (500/50/5), and chromaticity. Key gaps vs best methodologies: (a) absolute frequency thresholds don't scale with site size — a percentile-based threshold would be more defensible; (b) cross-page consistency (Step 9 in cluster.ts) is computed but NOT fed into stability scoring — best methodologies use it as a primary signal; (c) weighting is hardcoded without calibration against known design systems.

**[P1] Step 5 — Functional vs Evocative Naming** — Methodologies that avoid fabrication use functional/positional names: 'color used as 88% of borders' instead of 'Nobel Navy'. Our color_role_taxonomy.md already provides a functional taxonomy (primary, secondary, accent, background, surface, text-primary, border, focus, destructive, success, warning, info, highlight, overlay — 19 functional roles). This IS the anti-fabrication discipline. However, the writing_style_guide.md §6 mandates a 'personality sentence' on every color entry (e.g. 'A saturated blue-violet that reads as financially authoritative') — re-introducing fabrication. The gold-standard examples MIX both: 'Stripe Purple' (evocative name) + functional role + personality sentence.

**[P2] Step 3 — OKLCH Clustering Threshold** — The OKLCH clustering uses deltaE < 3 with SCALED Euclidean distance (dl×100, dc×100, dh with √(a·b) product). The effective perceptual threshold varies across OKLCH space: near-achromatic colors at high L would merge at visually distinct values; saturated mid-L colors would separate at visually similar values. The methodology flaw: fixed threshold but non-uniform perceptual meaning.

**[P2] Step 6 — Narrative Scope Consensus** — The design_md_format.md mandates narrative prose across 14+ sections — personality sentences, named principles, comparative framing, metaphors, vibe paragraphs. The consensus in published methodology (context.dev + similar) is that a design-system doc should be value-tables + minimal factual annotation at the token level, reserving interpretive narrative for the system-level introduction. Our format spec is the OPPOSITE. The gold-standard examples follow the narrative-heavy pattern but were authored by the same LLM methodology — self-reinforcing, not independently validated. The fabrication-safe consensus: §0 Brand Context + §1 Visual Theme → narrative appropriate; §2-§17 → value-tables with factual annotations only.

Style Dictionary renders programmatic output; Token Studio auto-generates documentation from token metadata. Guarantee: HIGH for table sections; LOW for prose sections. **[P0] Invert primary artifact: tokens.json → deterministic DESIGN.md (doc-as-token-view)** — Every analyzed platform (Supernova, Backlight, Zeroheight, Knapsack) renders docs FROM tokens as live-bound views — the doc is a projection of data, not a free-write. Current pipeline has AI WRITE the entire 17-section DESIGN.md including value-tables (colors, typography, shadows, radii, spacing), which is the primary hallucination vector: validate.ts catches phantom hex codes but NOT fabricated fontSize/fontWeight/lineHeight values. Diez DocsGen generates token documentation deterministically from typed token data with zero inference. The fix: split WRITE into (1) AUTO-RENDER: deterministic formatters.ts generates Sections 2-8 as markdown tables directly from tokens.json (zero AI), (2) AI-ANNOTATE: AI writes only narrative sections (0, 1, 5, 7, 8, 9-16) where judgment is required. tokens.json becomes the source-of-truth primary deliverable; DESIGN.md becomes a rendered view. DTCG $type/$value wrapping of tokens.json unblocks type-gated validation + downstream emit interop (Style Dictionary, Terrazzo, Theo all consume DTCG natively).

**[P1] Cite-the-token guardrail + evidence quotas for AI prose sections** — extract-design-system avoids hallucination by never asking the LLM to write prose — output is structured data only. Dembrandt is pure CLI with zero AI. Our hybrid architecture (CLI extract → AI write → CLI validate) keeps AI prose as value proposition but the WRITE prompt (design_md_prompt_template.md) has only 6 cardinal rules with weak anti-fabrication. Current Sections 1 (Visual Theme: 200-300 words of LLM-invented metaphors), 7 (Content & Voice: 'vibe paragraph' from LLM interpretation), and 0 (Brand Context: LLM-selected personality adjectives) are high-fabrication-risk. The fix: every prose claim must parenthetically cite >=2 token values. Every named design principle must cite >=3 token paths from tokens.json. Section 7's 'vibe paragraph' replaced with observed-data-only format (actual UI text samples, tone derived ONLY from tokens).

**[P1] Emit tokens.css alongside tokens.json (CSS custom properties output)** — extract-design-system (arvindrk) emits tokens.json + tokens.css as dual output — tokens.css is a :root block of CSS custom properties that agents can @import directly. The transform from tokens.json→tokens.css is trivial (iterate tokens, emit --color-primary: #533afd; etc.). Open Design's design system format already uses tokens.css as its structured token deliverable (proven in anobel.com/004-bento-visuals: 120+ CSS custom properties covering brand colors, ramps, typography, radii, surfaces). CSS custom properties are directly consumable by any frontend agent without JSON parsing — the agent just references var(--color-primary). This gives agents a zero-friction import path while DESIGN.md remains the human-readable documentation.

**[P2] Dual-layer document: structured summaries atop prose sections** — Open Design's DESIGN.md is consumed by 4+ coding agents with a DETERMINISTIC RE-RENDER schema — same input produces same output regardless of which agent reads it. Our prose-heavy sections (1, 7, 0) produce different interpretations per agent: Claude Code might extract 'shadow-as-border' as a technique; Codex might extract it as a design philosophy. Section 13 (Agent Prompt Guide) already proves structured consumption works: self-contained prompts work identically for any agent. The fix for remaining prose sections: add a 3-5 line key-value 'Agent-Oriented Summary' block at the top of each prose-heavy section, with parseable key-value pairs that any agent can consume without NLP.

Lower priority because our agents already read tokens.json directly in practice, and the P0 deterministic-tables change eliminates most prose-trust issues for value sections. A CLI fallback (query-token.ts --key 'colors.primary' --format json) provides 80% of the value without MCP infrastructure.

**[P0] adopt DTCG $type/$value schema** — SCOPED — HIGH-disruption migration with MEDIUM anti-fabrication return. The core prose fabrication problem (inventing 'gradient-as-depth' philosophy, 'consistent focus indicators', vibe paragraphs) is NOT caused by token type absence — it's caused by the WRITE phase being instructed to produce interpretive prose with no evidence quotas. DTCG $type/$value creates structural provenance (a shadow claim must reference a $type:'shadow' token) which enables type-gated validation, but it does NOT prevent the AI from writing 'The gradient creates atmospheric depth' — that's a prose control issue solved by evidence quotas + deterministic table generation, not token typing. The REAL value of DTCG adoption is downstream interoperability (Style Dictionary, Tokens Studio consumers) and cleaner validator architecture. The cost is substantial: ~200 LOC in cluster.ts for DTCG wrapping, new interfaces in types.ts, validate.ts refactoring to type-gated checks, and a breaking change to tokens.json format. Migration should be phased: (1) evidence quotas + deterministic tables first (higher anti-fabrication ROI), (2) DTCG wrapping as a parallel output (tokens.dtcg.json alongside tokens.json), (3) full migration once the format stabilizes.

**[P0] cut 17 sections → 9 / demote 5 required sections to conditional** — REAL — the claim that 5 required sections are never written in practice is verified. Iteration 018 audited the 4 gold-standard examples: Vercel outputs 10 sections, Stripe 9, Linear 9, Supabase ~9. The format spec (design_md_format.md §1 table, lines 27-48) marks 15 sections as required (0-13 + 6.5), but validate.ts:244-260 enforces all 15. The sections that are frequently absent from actual outputs are: §0 Brand Context, §7 Content & Voice, §11 State Matrix, §12 Iconography, and often §9 Accessibility Contract. These sections have zero or near-zero backing token data in single-page extractions — the validator REQUIRES their headers (checkSectionCompleteness at line 241-285 is a pure string-match for `## N. SectionName`), forcing the AI to fabricate content. The spec should honestly reflect what the pipeline CAN produce: core 9-10 sections (1-6, 6.5, 8, 10, 13) as REQUIRED, and demote 0, 7, 9, 11, 12 to CONDITIONAL (only when backed by ≥N tokens). This reduces fabrication pressure by removing 'must produce' obligations for data-poor sections.

**[P0] focus-consistent hard check** — REAL — the root-cause line still exists at a11y-extract.ts:101-103: `if (focusStyles.length === 0) { return { style: {}, consistent: true }; }`. This returns consistent:true when interaction capture was never run (it's OFF by default per extract.ts:81 `noInteraction = true`). The fix is clean and low-false-positive: add a `captured: boolean` field on A11yTokens.focusIndicator (types.ts:271-274), change the zero-length branch to return `{ style: null, consistent: null, captured: false }`, and add a gate in validate.ts that flags any §9 Focus Indicators prose beyond 'Not observed' when captured===false. No legitimate case produces zero focusStyles with captured:true.

**[P1] checkProseClaims validator — banned interpretive phrases** — SCOPED — adopt with two caveats. Caveat 1: banned-word lists are inherently brittle (e.g., 'robust' can describe a validation pipeline legitimately; 'clean' can describe a separation of concerns). The numeric-anchor suppression window (±50 chars) mitigates but doesn't eliminate false positives — MUST be WARNING-tier only, never hard-failure, with weight ≤1pt. Caveat 2: the inline `<!-- source: tokens.x -->` sentinel mechanism IS implementable without burdening the writer — stripHtmlComments() already exists at validate.ts:51-53, HTML-comment parsing is a ~30 LOC addition to validate.ts — but it should be a separate structural check, not bundled with the banned-word heuristic. The claims.json sidecar was correctly rejected by iteration 013 (C8) as a desync risk.

**[P1] coverage-based token election over OKLCH+L1-L4** — SCOPED — additive, not replacement. Coverage-based election (page-level prevalence as primary gate) would NOT regress current behavior — it would CORRECT current misclassifications. The current classifyColorStability (cluster.ts:400-435) uses a 5-signal additive score where pageCoverage contributes only ±30 of a max ~120. A one-off hero gradient on 1 of 8 pages (12.5% coverage) but with high element-frequency (500+), a CSS variable, and multi-dimensional usage can score ≥30 and reach L2 (system). Coverage election as a HARD PRE-GATE (token on <30% of pages → capped at L3 regardless of other signals) would correctly demote these one-off campaign tokens. However, coverage election does NOT replace the OKLCH+L1-L4 scoring — it precedes it. The OKLCH clustering (deltaE<3 for perceptual merging) and the 5-signal stability scoring (pagesCoverage, usage dimensions, CSS var, frequency, chromaticity) still provide value within each coverage band: they distinguish infrastructure vs system vs campaign WITHIN the set of multi-page tokens. The coverage gate prevents single-page tokens from leaking into the stability classifier at all, solving the root-cause misclassification while preserving the nuanced scoring for multi-page tokens.

**[P1] named-principle must bind to a token / make philosophy conditional-on-evidence** — SCOPED — removing mandatory philosophy entirely WOULD lose genuine value. When a named principle is grounded in actual tokens (e.g., 'Shadow-as-border' with `0px 0px 0px 1px rgba(0,0,0,0.08)` appearing 4x more than elevation shadows), it communicates the WHY behind the data and is the single most useful part of a design doc for an implementing developer. The adversarial concern is valid: iteration 021's proposal to quarantine ALL narrative to §0-§1 only would throw out genuinely valuable, evidence-backed principles along with the fabricated ones. The balance is: make named principles CONDITIONAL ON EVIDENCE — require ≥3 distinct backing tokens across ≥2 pages before a principle may be named. When evidence is thin, stamp ABSENT. This preserves the high-value principles while eliminating fabrication pressure. Evidence from iteration 006 (C3): the format spec's unconditional 'Named principle — state the depth philosophy upfront' clause IS the fabrication driver, not the principle concept itself.

L4 tokens are hard-excluded from DESIGN.md; L3 tokens require "Subject to change" annotation. The validator enforces this via checkStabilityGating() (validate.ts:100-123) — any L4 hex appearing in DESIGN.md is a critical failure regardless of score.

**[P0] cluster.ts:752,911 — per-token confidence from coverage-election** — ColorToken.confidence and TypographyLevel.confidence already computed as 'high'|'medium'|'low' from frequency thresholds (≤2=low, ≥5=high) and page-coverage ratio (≥0.5=high). This is pure coverage-election: a color found on 1 page 2 times is low-confidence; a color found on 80% of pages 500+ times is high. The field exists in the schema (types.ts:424,439) and is populated at extraction time, but the DESIGN.md writer does not currently surface it. Fidelity mechanism: deterministic, runs on element-frequency histograms + crawled-page intersection, no ML, no guesswork.

**[P0] validate.ts:57-93,318-349 — graded validator with critical-failure gating** — The validator already produces a graded score (0-100: -5 per failure, -1 per warning) AND enforces CRITICAL_FAILURE_TYPES (phantom-color, content-color, missing-section) that hard-fail regardless of score. A single fabricated hex triggers FAIL even if all other checks pass. This is already an evidence-completeness validator, not purely binary: it distinguishes between format issues (warnings: -1) and fabrication (failures: -5, gate-failing). The score degradations per-error type create a natural severity scale.

**[P1] a11y-extract.ts:257-357 — measured WCAG data, no aggregate grade** — The a11y extractor computes real WCAG contrast ratios from DOM color pairs (not templated), checks AA/AAA per pair, measures min touch-target sizes, detects focus-indicator consistency, and checks reduced-motion support. This is measured, transcribable data — every number traces to a real DOM element. However, no aggregate a11y grade is computed. The data sits in tokens.json a11yTokens but §9 of DESIGN.md gets raw data, not a synthesized grade.

**[P1] proof.ts:75-100 — fidelity pixel-sampling coverage** — The proof module captures a live screenshot, samples 2,000 pixels from CSS-rendered areas (excluding img/video/svg/background-image regions), converts each to OKLCH, and computes delta-E perceptual distance against the extracted palette. Pixels within deltaE<12 count as matched. This produces a single coverage percentage (e.g., 94.2%) — a measured answer to "how much of the visual surface does our palette explain?" Low coverage signals the extractor missed tokens or the site has extensive image-driven color.

**[P2] design-boundary-detect.ts:228-311 — cross-site similarity scores per dimension** — The boundary detector computes similarity scores per dimension (font, color, spacing, radius, component, shadow) between site groups. These are inherently confidence signals: a site with a single page scores 100 on everything (no variance = low evidence of system-ness). Multi-domain extractions produce real similarity scores that reveal where a design system is consistent vs. fragmented — the thin dimensions signal invention-prone areas.

**[P0] fix** — TIER-1: deltaE<3 under-clusters brand colors by ~10×, corrupts stability classification. The ×100-scaled OKLCH deltaE threshold of 3 (cluster.ts:708) is equivalent to unscaled perceptual difference <0.03 — far tighter than the 1-3 DE2000 range for perceptible difference. Brand colors like #3B82F6 and #60A5FA (deltaE≈8.1 at this scale) do NOT merge. The greedy single-pass loop (cluster.ts:703-724) sorts by frequency descending and commits to first match with no recentering. Each split fragment gets diluted frequency and pagesCoverage (per-page freq divided by pages at cluster.ts:741), pulling stability below L1 cutoff. Starves: §2 Color Palette — brand colors split into L4 content tier and excluded, AI invents missing brand colors.

**[P0] fix** — TIER-1: extractA11yAsync dead code + cssAnalyses not passed — 5 §9 fields always null. extractA11yAsync (a11y-extract.ts:361-391) returns tabOrder, langAttribute, skipLinkDetected, altTextCoverage but is never imported (extract.ts:19 misses it) and never called. Only sync extractA11y is called at extract.ts:445. Additionally, cssAnalyses is not passed as 3rd arg to extractA11y (a11y-extract.ts:260 accepts optional param), so reducedMotionSupport is always undefined despite extractReducedMotionSupport helper existing (a11y-extract.ts:344-347). Starves: §9 Accessibility — tabOrder, lang, skipLink, altTextCoverage, plus reducedMotion for §6.5.

**[P0] fix** — TIER-1: Interaction pipeline broken-by-design — default OFF + component-type key mismatch. extract.ts:81 defaults noInteraction=true, --fast forces it, so captureInteractions never runs (extract.ts:322). When opt-in via --with-interaction, findInteraction (cluster.ts:1294) constructs lookup key with el.tag ('a'/'div') while interactionLookup (cluster.ts:1288) uses capture.componentType from classifyComponent ('link'/'button'). Mismatch drops ALL interaction data for <a> tags and role-based components. Starves: §11 State Matrix (5 state columns × all components → entirely fabricated), §4 Component Stylings (hover/focus/active/disabled lines with rationale → all null), §9 Focus Indicators (style always {}) — PLUS loading/empty/error states never populated.

**[P0] fix** — TIER-1: No token→section coverage map exists — all section starvation is mechanically undetectable. validate.ts:241-285 (checkSectionCompleteness) checks whether 15-17 section headers exist in markdown text, not whether backing tokens exist. validate.ts:57-93 (checkPhantomColors) detects hex colors not in tokens — color-level, not section-level. report-gen.ts, preview-gen.ts, and proof.ts serve other purposes. Starves: all 17 sections — enables invisible fabrication at scale.

**[P1] fix** — TIER-2: classifyShadow duplicated with drift risk. Defined twice in cluster.ts: exported version at line 374-396 and inline duplicate at line 1047-1072 inside clusterTokens. The inline version is the one called at line 1090 for shadowTokens classification. If a maintainer fixes a bug in the exported version, the inline copy silently drifts. Both implementations are currently identical, so no present correctness impact. Zero-blur border-shadows correctly separated because spread>0 gates within both copies.

**[P1] fix** — TIER-2: classifyVariant default-to-Primary masks real variant diversity + hard RGB Destructive heuristic false-positives. Fallthrough `return 'Primary'` at cluster.ts:1256 captures: light bg + light text without shadow/border (should be Ghost-on-light or Outline), medium-brightness bg with any text (should be Tertiary), dark bg + dark text (unclassified). Flattens Outline/Tertiary/Link/Subtle into Primary → DESIGN.md over-reports Primary. Hard RGB check at cluster.ts:1245 (`bg.r>180 && bg.g<100 && bg.b<100`) only detects red-spectrum backgrounds: misses non-red destructive (amber/orange) and false-positives when red IS the brand color.

**[P1] fix** — TIER-2: colorRelationships.contrastPairs capped at top-20 high-frequency colors for §2 Color Relationships. cluster.ts:799 filters frequency>=5 then .slice(0,20), computing pairwise contrast among those 20 only. Real text-on-bg pairs involving lower-frequency but semantically important colors (e.g., a muted-label color used across all pages at frequency=4) are excluded from colorRelationships view. Note: §9 a11y contrast data is NOT capped — extractA11y at a11y-extract.ts:265-304 computes uncapped DOM element pairs, and extract.ts:469 overwrites cluster's a11yTokens with those. The cap ONLY affects §2 colorRelationships.contrastPairs.

**[P1] fix** — TIER-2: Component geometric heuristics are site-specific and miss common variants. Badge (cluster.ts:1191): `radiusPx>=100` requires fully-pill border-radius — misses badges with radius 8-24px, `height<30` misses 32-40px status badges, `hasBg` misses border-only badges. Card (cluster.ts:1204-1214): `width 200-800` excludes sidebar cards (<200px) and full-width dashboard cards (>800px), `padding>=12` excludes compact cards (8-10px common in dense UIs), `childrenCount>=2` is the most fragile — `

Text

` has childrenCount=1 and is skipped entirely, which is the single most common card pattern. Starves: §4 component list and §11 card variants.

**[P1] fix** — TIER-2: Frequency underweighted in stability scoring — L4 content-derived colors leak to L2. classifyColorStability (cluster.ts:400-435) caps frequency at ±20 of 120 max score, while pagesCoverage (+30), text/border usage (+25), and cssVar (+20) alone can reach 75. A one-off color on 50%+ pages bound to a CSS variable: pagesCoverage +20 + cssVar +20 + text/border +25 + freq≤5 −20 = 45 ≥ 30 → L2 (system). The SKILL.md contract requires L4 = excluded entirely, but this token enters DESIGN.md as system-tier, enabling fabrication from a content-derived value.

**[P1] fix** — TIER-2: Hidden-element skew in minTouchTarget/minFontSize. The shipping code (a11y-extract.ts:306-338) has reasonable guards (interactive tags filter, zero-area exclusion, textContent check) but both implementations lack visibility filtering. A 1px-wide interactive element hidden via CSS (display:none, visibility:hidden, opacity:0, off-screen) can produce a misleading global minimum. The dead-code cluster.ts:1532-1568 version is worse: minFontSize scans ALL elements with positive parsePxValue without checking textContent or visibility. Starves: §9 touch target data (WCAG false violations), minFontSize claims.

**[P1] fix** — TIER-2: Single-page motion extraction discards keyframes from pages 2+. extract.ts:439 passes `cssAnalyses[0]` — only first page's CSS analysis — to extractMotion. extractMotion (motion-extract.ts:95-173) iterates that single analysis's transitions and animations. If page 2+ contains unique @keyframes (common for subpage-specific hero animations) or distinct timing functions, they are silently discarded. §6.5 Motion System requires duration scale, easing functions, and keyframe animations — thin data forces AI to invent a motion philosophy from partial observations.

**[P2] fix** — TIER-3: Architectural gaps — 4 sections have zero extraction tokens. §0 Brand Context, §1 Visual Theme & Atmosphere, §7 Content & Voice, §8 Do's and Don'ts require semantic/interpretive data (adjectives, tone descriptors, rules) but DesignTokens has no fields for them. Additionally: §11 global loading/empty/error states detected as page-level patterns (interaction-capture.ts:394-690) with no component-type binding; §10 responsive collapsing has no multi-viewport diff; §12 iconography has no icon-to-text adjacency analysis. These are architectural gaps requiring new extraction capabilities, not simple bug fixes.

**[P2] fix** — TIER-3: Breakpoints lack per-breakpoint layout diffs (keyChanges). MediaBreakpoint (types.ts:153-158) carries only {query, type, value, ruleCount}. Extraction runs single-viewport DOM at 1440px (extract.ts:298) — all element sizing comes from one width. cluster.ts:1487-1504 deduplicates breakpoints by type:value summing ruleCount only. §10 'Collapsing Strategy' requires observing how elements change size/layout across breakpoints (e.g. 'Hero headline: 56px→44px at 1024px'). No multi-viewport element comparison exists. §10 pixel-transition claims are entirely fabricated.

**[P2] fix** — TIER-3: Gradients stored as raw CSS strings with no structural decomposition. GradientInfo (types.ts:124-130) stores only {type, value, elementTag, elementClasses, rect}. cluster.ts:1476-1481 aggregates into {type, value, elementTag, location} — never decomposes into angle, stop positions, or stop colors. extractGradientColors (cluster.ts:244-261) regex-extracts color substrings only. No GradientToken with decomposed stops[{color,position}] exists. §6 Depth & Elevation 'Decorative Depth' subsection requires parsed gradient data. The AI writer must regex-parse raw strings to extract angles/stops, enabling invention.

**[P1] C-ROUTE-FIDELITY-AUDIT** — The smart router (§2) has four intents: EXTRACT_WRITE, VALIDATE, REPORT, STUDY. VALIDATE checks hex accuracy and section structure — purely structural. **[P0] Coverage-based token election (additive pre-gate)** — design-extract (Manavarya09, 3.3k stars). Iteration-024 verdict: SCOPED-additive. Current classifyColorStability (cluster.ts:400-435) uses 5-signal additive score where pageCoverage contributes only ±30 of max ~120. A one-off hero gradient on 1/8 pages (12.5% coverage) with 500+ element-frequency can reach L2. Coverage election as HARD PRE-GATE (pagesCoverage <0.3 → cap at L3) prevents single-page token leakage. Adoption cost: LOW (~25 LOC pre-gate in cluster.ts:569).

**[P0] Deterministic value-table rendering (Sections 2-8 auto-render)** — Diez DocsGen, Supernova, Backlight, Zeroheight, Knapsack, Style Dictionary. Current pipeline AI-writes ALL 17 sections including pure value-tables (colors, typography, shadows, radii, spacing) — validate.ts catches phantom hex but NOT fabricated fontSize/fontWeight/lineHeight. Deterministic formatters eliminate the primary hallucination surface for numeric sections. Adoption cost: MEDIUM (~200 LOC new formatters.ts).

**[P0] Evidence-gated named principles (conditional on ≥3 tokens)** — Superposition/CSS Stats thin-evidence omission pattern. Iteration-024 verdict: SCOPED — evidence-gating, not abolition. Current format mandates named principles even with sparse data (AP-25 treats missing principle as defect). Iteration-024 confirmed evidence-backed principles (e.g., 'shadow-as-border' with real frequency data) ARE highest-value prose. Fix: require ≥3 distinct backing tokens across ≥2 pages. Adoption cost: LOW (~30 LOC in design_md_format.md + prompt template).

**[P0] Deterministic renderer (formatters.ts)** — The core architectural change: a TypeScript script at tool/scripts/formatters.ts that reads tokens.json (post-cluster, post-DTCG-wrap) and emits markdown for all class-(a) sections. This runs BEFORE the AI WRITE phase. The AI then receives pre-rendered sections as its input and is contractually forbidden from modifying them — it only appends class-(b) annotation lines and class-(c) [INFERRED] prose blocks. This structurally removes the LLM from value-table generation, eliminating the primary hallucination vector that validate.ts currently cannot catch: fabricated fontSize (e.g., 14px typed as 16px), fabricated fontWeight (400 typed as 500), fabricated lineHeight, fabricated shadow offsets/blurs, fabricated border-radius values. validate.ts only checks hex colors against tokens.json (checkPhantomColors at line 57) — all other token types pass unchecked.

**[P0] Three-class section partition** — Sections partitioned into DETERMINISTIC (auto-rendered value tables, zero AI), GROUNDED-ANNOTATION (value table + 1-line token-cited AI note), and BOUNDED-PROSE (labeled [INFERRED], gated/optional). Class (a) covers §2 Colors, §2.5 Dark Mode, §3 Typography, §5 Spacing+Radii, §6 Shadow table, §6.5 Duration/Easing tables, §9 Contrast table, §10 Breakpoints, §12 Icon sizing, §17 Token Dictionary — sections where every value has a tokens.json row. This removes the LLM as a value-invention surface for fontSize/fontWeight/lineHeight/shadow/radius fabrication that validate.ts currently cannot catch (only hex values are checked). Class (b) covers §4 Component Stylings, §5 Whitespace Philosophy, §6 Named Principle, §6.5 Motion Philosophy+Choreography, §8 Do's/Don'ts, §11 State Matrix — the AI writes 1-line annotations that MUST cite token values. Class (c) covers §0 Brand Context, §1 Visual Theme, §7 Content & Voice, §13 Prompt Guide, §14-16 — AI prose labeled [INFERRED], each paragraph citing >=2 token values.

**[P0] Token-citation gating in validate.ts** — For class-(b) annotations and class-(c) prose: every AI-written claim must parenthetically cite >=2 token values from tokens.json. The current validate.ts (lines 57-350) has no citation-awareness — it checks hex provenance (phantom colors) and format consistency but never verifies that prose claims are grounded in token evidence. A new validation gate, checkCitationGating(), parses citation annotations in markdown (e.g., 'Light weight as signature: Weight 300 at 56px [token: typographyLevels[0].weight, §3]') and verifies each cited token path exists in tokens.json. This closes the gap that iteration-018 identified: Section 1's 'named principles' and Section 7's 'vibe paragraph' currently have zero evidence-quota enforcement.

**[P1] Composition with TIER-1 fixes into coherent target** — Three existing TIER-1 fixes compose directly into the doc-as-view architecture: (1) focus-check (hex-format validation at validate.ts:181-200) remains as post-generation gate for class-(c) AI-written sections where hex values may still appear in prose — but becomes a second-line defense since class-(a) sections are auto-generated from tokens.json with guaranteed format. (2) section-cut (section completeness at validate.ts:241-285) is updated to reflect three-class partitioning: class-(a) sections are ALWAYS present (formatters guarantee), class-(b) sections are REQUIRED when tokens contain relevant data, class-(c) sections are OPTIONAL with a [SKIPPED: insufficient token data] marker when token data is below threshold. (3) AP-29 / stability-gating (checkStabilityGating at validate.ts:100-123) blocks L4 content colors from appearing in any section — this is critical for class-(a) auto-rendered tables where formatters must apply the same L1/L2 filter as the cluster before rendering. The target architecture adds a pre-render stability filter that formatters.ts applies before generating any table.

**[P1] DTCG-typed tokens.json spine** — Migration of tokens.json from flat custom schema to W3C DTCG 2025.10 format ($type, $value, $description per token) enables: (a) type-gated validation — validate.ts can check that a DTCG-typed token's $value matches what appears in DESIGN.md for $type=duration (not just $type=color), (b) deterministic formatters can render $type-appropriate markdown (color → hex table, dimension → px+rem scale, duration → ms table, cubicBezier → CSS function), (c) $description fields provide prose anchors for class-(b) annotations and class-(c) claims, and (d) downstream interop with Style Dictionary, Terrazzo, Theo. This is the structural backbone that makes the three-class partition enforceable at the type level rather than convention.

**[P2] Migration path: minimal vs full** — Minimal viable first step (Phase A): formatters.ts generates only §2 Color Palette and §3 Typography tables — the two highest hallucination-risk value sections that together carry ~40% of fabricated values. Keeps the rest of the WRITE phase unchanged (AI still writes §5 Spacing, §6 Shadows, etc. as prose). Adds citation-gating to validate.ts for just §1 and §7. This eliminates ~60% of value-fabrication surface with ~20% of the full implementation effort. Full architecture (Phase B): all class-(a) sections auto-rendered, DTCG-typed tokens.json, full citation gating across all class-(b)/(c) sections, tokens.css emission, and structured summaries atop prose sections. The migration is additive — Phase A outputs are forward-compatible because formatters produce the same markdown structure as the format spec; the AI can still write alongside auto-rendered tables.

**[P2] tokens.css emission as secondary artifact** — Trivial transform from tokens.json→tokens.css as a :root CSS custom properties block. extract-design-system (arvindrk) proves this dual-output pattern. CSS custom properties are directly importable by agents without JSON parsing — agents reference var(--color-primary) directly. This makes DESIGN.md the human-readable documentation while tokens.css is the machine-consumable token source, closing the multi-agent consumption gap identified in iteration-018 (§4). The transform is low-risk because it's read-only over already-validated tokens.

**[P0] Frequency-only scoring allows content to masquerade as system** — classifyColorStability Signal 4 (cluster.ts:419-421): frequency >= 50 adds +20 regardless of page coverage. A color appearing 200 times on a single page (hero section, gradient stops, repeated card bg) gets +20. Combined with achromatic +10, that's +30, minus -20 (single-page) minus -15 (bg-only) = -5 → 'campaign'. But if it's bg+border (2 dimensions), the -15 vanishes: score = +30 - 20 = +10 → 'system'. A single-page, 2-dimension achromatic color at freq=50 becomes a 'system' token. Cross-page prevalence is the missing hard gate.

**[P0] Post-hoc classification without prevalence gate leaks one-off content** — classifyColorStability (cluster.ts:400-435) scores colors AFTER all are collected and clustered. A single-page, bg-only gray like #646464 scores: freq>=50 → +10, achromatic → +10, pages<0.2 → -20, bg-only → -15 = -15 → 'content'. BUT a single-page gradient color with freq=60 scores: freq>=50 → +10, gradientColor-only → -15, single-page → -20 = -25 → 'content'. The real leak is upstream: these values enter clustering at line 698-724, get merged into cluster representatives, and inflate token counts + color relationship scales BEFORE stability is ever checked. The classification labels them 'content' but they already corrupted the system palette. Coverage-based election (design-extract, Superposition) filters BEFORE clustering — a value on 1 page never enters the token set.

**[P1] design-boundary-detect.ts operates on page-groups, not individual tokens** — detectBoundaries (design-boundary-detect.ts:228-311) compares PageGroups pairwise, answering 'do these page sets share a design system?' It does NOT answer 'is this specific value a design token or content?' The scoreColorOverlap (line 92-107) uses raw hex lists with rgbDistance < 15 threshold — it sees ALL colors including content. A page group with 200 content colors dilutes the overlap signal. The boundary detection and the stability classification are solving different problems with no shared signal.

**[P1] OKLCH clustering merges content colors into system clusters** — The greedy clustering at cluster.ts:700-724 sorts by frequency descending and merges any color within deltaE < 3 of an existing cluster. A high-frequency system blue (#0066CC, freq=500) can absorb a one-off content teal (#0077AA, freq=1) if they're within deltaE 3. The merged cluster inherits the system blue's frequency + pages, making the content teal invisible. Superposition-style pre-filtering would prevent this: unused/rare colors never enter the merge pool.

**[P1] Pipeline order: classify-after-cluster loses provenance** — Current pipeline: collect → cluster (deltaE<3) → build tokens → classify stability. The cluster step merges pages sets and frequencies, so a 1-page content color merged into a 5-page system color inherits pagesCoverage=5/N. classifyColorStability then sees 5 pages and scores +30 for coverage. The original single-page provenance is erased. Running a prevalence filter BEFORE clustering (Superposition-style) preserves the boundary signal.

**[P2] Hybrid design: prevalence-gate + perceptual-cluster + role-by-usage** — Optimal 3-stage pipeline: (1) PREVALENCE GATE — value must appear on >=2 pages OR have frequency>=20 across >=2 usage dimensions. Eliminates ~60-80% of content values cheaply. (2) PERCEPTUAL CLUSTER — deltaE<3 greedy merge on survivors only. No content colors contaminate system clusters. (3) ROLE-BY-USAGE — classifyColorStability runs on filtered+clustered tokens. With content already removed, the scoring signals (CSS vars, achromaticity, structural tags) become reliable discriminators between infrastructure/system/campaign rather than fighting noise. This mirrors Superposition (exclude unused) + CSS Stats (cluster by occurrence) + our OKLCH stability in sequence.

**[P0] Typography role naming without provenance** — §3 Typography Rules 'Hierarchy' table (design_md_format.md §6, line 396) demands a 'Role' column with invented names ('Display Hero', 'Body Large'). **[P1] Color naming without typed aliasing** — §2 Color Palette & Roles requires 'Descriptive brand-specific name, never Blue 1' and a 'Personality sentence' per color (design_md_format.md §4, line 181). ColorToken (types.ts:409-426) has hex, rgba, frequency, usedAs breakdown, cssVariableNames[], and sourcePages — but no name or role field. **[P1] ShadowToken.value as flat box-shadow string** — ShadowToken (types.ts:443-449) stores shadow as a single string value (e.g., 'rgba(50,50,93,0.12) 0px 16px 32px 0px'). §6 Depth & Elevation (design_md_format.md §9) requires the writer to classify shadows by type (Ring, Elevation, Inset) and describe them in a shadow scale table with Level/Treatment/Frequency/Use columns. **[P2] additive typed layer vs full DTCG rewrite** — Iteration-024 judged a full DTCG migration as SCOPED/high-cost because it would require rewriting every extraction path (cluster.ts ~1742 lines, multiple specialized parsers), every validator (validate.ts ~436 lines), and every MD generator pipeline. The additive path adds: (a) 1 field per interface ($type), (b) 1 field per token (id), (c) a projection function dtcgProject(tokens: DesignTokens): DTCGView that returns a typed alias layer without touching the extractors. This is ~80 lines of new code vs. a ~3000+ line rewrite. The provenance benefit (validator can cite token-ids, claim-to-token traceability) is delivered entirely by (a)+(b). The deterministic rendering benefit (§3 typography table, §6 shadow table, §6.5 motion tables) is delivered by (c) plus roleLabel/shadowLevelLabel fields. Full DTCG replacement can be deferred indefinitely.

**[P0] deltaE threshold <3 — SOUND (bug confirmed)** — cluster.ts:708: `if (deltaE(color.oklch, existing.oklch) < 3)`. The deltaE function (cluster.ts:348-354) scales OKLCH by ×100: Euclidean distance in a space where L∈[0,1], C∈[0,~0.4], H∈[0,360]. At ×100 scaling, ΔE=3 corresponds to perceptual difference ≈0.03 — roughly 10× tighter than the human visual threshold (ΔE≈2-3 CIEDE2000 maps to ~30-50 in this scaled space). iteration-031:#3 cites #3B82F6 and #60A5FA (ΔE≈8.1) — these are visually distinct blues that should NOT merge. The greedy single-pass at cluster.ts:703-724 commits to first match with no recentering (line 711-713 merges into the cluster representative without recomputing the centroid). The pages-size division at line 738 flattens per-page frequency into a distributed average. All three claims verified against source. The same bug exists at the merge path at cluster.ts:1664-1667 (also uses <3).

Also: cluster.ts:1558-1568 contains a dead-code duplicate a11y computation (focusStyle/focusConsistent/minTouchWidth/minTouchHeight) that is overwritten by extract.ts:469 (`tokens.a11yTokens = a11yTokens`) before output. **[P0] validator prose checks — SOUND-WITH-CAVEAT** — All 6 validate.ts checks (lines 323-345) are mechanical: phantom-color (hex-tracing), stability-gating (L4 exclusion), unknown-font (font family), format-consistency (hex case/weight format), section-completeness (header string match), content-checks (typo-table presence + color-count). NONE reads prose semantics. A doc scoring 99/100 can be full of 'A sleek, modern design with elegant depth principles.' The plan proposes checkProseDiscipline with BANNED_ADJECTIVES + numeric-anchor suppression (±50 chars). iteration-024:#1 correctly scoped this as WARNING-tier only (weight ≤1pt), not hard-failure — because words like 'robust' and 'clean' have legitimate non-fabrication uses. The SELF-REVIEW step (classify each sentence ANCHORED vs INTERPRETIVE) is a prompt-template change, not a code change — its effectiveness depends entirely on the LLM's willingness to self-critique accurately, which is unreliable without an adversarial second pass.

**[P1] deterministic renderer citation gap — SOUND-WITH-CAVEAT** — The architecture correctly partitions §2,§2.5,§3,§5(spacing+radii),§6(shadow table),§6.5(duration+easing),§9(contrast),§10(breakpoints),§12(sizing),§17 as class-(a) DETERMINISTIC — every value in these sections has a tokens.json row. The formatters.ts approach (iteration-030:#2) would structurally prevent value fabrication for these sections. BUT the plan's citation-gating (checkCitationGating) verifies that `[token: <path>]` markers resolve to real tokens — it does NOT verify the TRUTH of the AI's claim about the token. Example: the AI writes 'The gradient creates atmospheric depth [token: gradients[2].value, §6]' — checkCitationGating confirms gradients[2].value EXISTS, but cannot verify that the gradient 'creates atmospheric depth.' This is a fundamental limitation: citation gating proves grounding, not correctness. For class-(b) annotations (§4,§5 whitespace philosophy,§6 named principle,§6.5 motion philosophy,§8,§11), the AI must interpret token data into prose — no deterministic renderer can replace this judgment. The format spec §6 (design_md_format.md:544) requires the AI to NAME a depth philosophy ('Chromatic depth', 'Shadow-as-border', etc.) — this classification act requires AI judgment about PATTERNS in token data, not just citation of token values. The deterministic renderer produces tables; the AI must still interpret what those tables MEAN.

**[P1] findInteraction component key mismatch — SOUND** — cluster.ts:1293-1303: findInteraction builds key1 as `${el.tag}|${el.className}|${el.tag}` — the third component is el.tag, not the component TYPING. The lookup key at line 1288 is `${capture.element.tag}|${capture.element.classes}|${capture.componentType}` where componentType comes from classifyComponent() (interaction-capture.ts:118-126) and returns 'link' for <a> tags, 'button' for <button>, etc. The key mismatch: lookup uses el.tag (e.g., 'a') while the stored key uses componentType (e.g., 'link'). For <a> tags, these NEVER match — key1='a|...|a', stored='a|...|link' — interaction data is dropped. The fallback at lines 1297-1301 only matches by tag+partial class, ignoring component type entirely, and can false-match across component types. This is confirmed at the source level. The fix (use classifyComponent for key) is correct.

**[P1] interaction-default-ON performance — SOUND-WITH-CAVEAT** — PER_ELEMENT_TIMEOUT=6000ms (interaction-capture.ts:51), MAX_ELEMENTS=50 (line 53), PAGE_TIMEOUT=30000ms (line 52). The page budget hard-caps at 30s per page — NOT 5 minutes as the plan's own cited concern suggests. Worst case for 8-page crawl: 8 × 30s = 4 minutes for interaction capture. With 5 concurrency and pages already taking ~5-10s each for DOM+CSS, interaction capture roughly doubles total extraction time. The plan provides `--fast-no-interaction` opt-out (iteration-031:#10). However, the plan claims 'interaction capture adds ~5min worst-case per page' (iteration-031 line 34) — this is INACCURATE given the 30s PAGE_TIMEOUT. The real cost is bounded at 30s/page, which is significant but not prohibitive. No budget cap smaller than 30s is proposed — the 30s PAGE_TIMEOUT is already quite generous.

**[P1] regression risk (deltaE, coverage election) — SOUND-WITH-CAVEAT** — Two changes carry non-trivial regression risk: (1) deltaE threshold <3→<10: the example pair #3B82F6/#60A5FA (ΔE≈8.1) should NOT merge — these are perceptually different blues. At <10, legitimate accent-vs-primary color distinctions could collapse. The test file (cluster.test.ts:152-175) already validates threshold <3 as the correct boundary — changing to <10 without updating tests creates a silent deviation. (2) Coverage election pre-gate (pagesCoverage<0.3 → force L3): on 2-page sites, a token on 1 page (50% coverage) passes. On 3-page sites, 1/3 = 33% — threshold is 0.3, so 33% > 30%, passes. The edge case is exactly pagesCoverage=0.29 on sites with many pages — legitimate tokens could be demoted. (3) The anobel gold-standard example: cannot verify without running, but the deltaE change is the highest-risk change for altering color palette output. The format spec changes (comparative framing removal, named-principle conditional, motion OBSERVED/RECOMMENDED) carry zero regression risk — they REMOVE fabrication mandates, not data processing logic.

Recommendation: Accept the limitation explicitly: citation gating proves provenance, not truth. Document that class-(b) and class-(c) sections carry [INFERRED] labels and that downstream consumers should treat them as AI interpretations. For the named-principle classification act (design_md_format.md:544), consider making formatters.ts pre-compute candidate principle names from token patterns (e.g., 'shadow-as-border' when zero-blur shadows dominate) and present them as constrained options to the AI, rather than letting the AI invent names freely.

Recommendation: Before deploying, run the deltaE change against all 4 gold-standard examples (Vercel, Stripe, Linear, Supabase) and measure: (a) change in color token count, (b) any loss of visually distinct brand colors. Update cluster.test.ts with the new threshold and add regression tests for known distinct pairs. For coverage election, log L2→L3 demotions in the extraction report so users can audit edge cases. The anobel example should be explicitly tested before final sign-off.

Recommendation: Correct the '~5min per page' estimate in the plan — it's 30s per page max. Making interaction default-ON is safe with the 30s PAGE_TIMEOUT already in place. However, ADD `--fast-no-interaction` to the `--fast` flag definition (currently extract.ts:117-120 sets noInteraction=true without exposing an alternative), and document the time cost clearly in the CLI help text.

Recommendation: Demote §0,§7,§9,§11,§12 to conditional as proposed. The single highest-ROI change. Also fix the format spec's motion fallback (line 586-592): the 'consider adding' recommendation is an invention template that must be removed or labeled RECOMMENDED (per item 12). The comparative-framing mandate at line 130 must be removed per item 5.

Recommendation: Fix as described: change cluster.ts:1294 to `${el.tag}|${el.className}|${classifyComponent(el.tag, el.role)}`. Import classifyComponent from interaction-capture.ts or duplicate the function. The fallback direction at line 1298 should be inverted: try exact match first, then fall back to tag+partial-class, but ensure the componentType is also checked in the fallback to prevent cross-type false matches.

Recommendation: Implement as described. The captured:boolean sentinel is the cleanest possible fix — no false-positive path exists because zero focusStyles WITH interaction ON is logically impossible (at minimum the page body receives a focus event). Add a regression test: run extraction against a known site with focus styles, assert captured:true.

Recommendation: Implement banned-word check as WARNING-tier as agreed. The SELF-REVIEW step is the weak point — a single-pass LLM classifying its own sentences as INTERPRETIVE has low reliability. Consider instead a structural approach: require that every paragraph in class-(c) sections contain ≥2 parenthetical citations `[token: <path>]` — this is mechanically checkable without relying on LLM self-honesty. This citation-counting approach is PRECISELY what the plan proposes as checkCitationGating (iteration-031:#19), but that's deferred to full architecture. Move citation-gating up to minimal viable scope for class-(c) sections.

Recommendation: Raise threshold from <3 to <10 as proposed. ALSO fix the merge path at cluster.ts:1664 — currently uses the same <3 threshold and will silently drop incoming merge colors below that. The recommended threshold change must be applied in BOTH locations (cluster.ts:708 and cluster.ts:1664). The merge path is especially dangerous: it performs the same greedy clustering against an already-merged tokens.json, compounding the splitting error.
